• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Scripts for the analyses of the paper: Sex is the most attractive gender

Dr. Nour Efrat-Kowalsky

2025-11-14

Notes

This document contains the script to produce all analyses and plots for the paper “Sex is the most attractive gender:the birth and death of noun categorization in language. Authors: Carling, Gerd, Nour Efrat-Kowalsky, Niklas Erben-Johansson, Gerhard Jäger, Lev Michael, Marc Allassonnière-Tang, and Filip Larsson.

For the phylogenetic analyses, we take the world tree from Bouckaert et al. 2022 (https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/f8tr6/) and extract from it the languages that are in our sample for each family.

Basic settings

First, we read the basic packages and set the seed for reproducibility. The most relevant is the tidyverse work environment. See https://www.tidyverse.org/ for more details.

# load basic packages
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
# packages for correlation paired plot
library(GGally)
# packages for phylogenetics
library(ape)
library(phytools)
library(phangorn)
library(bayestraitr)
# packages for plots
library(diagram)
library(ggpubr)
library(ggtext)
library(patchwork)
# set seed for reproducibility 
set.seed(10)
# set visual options for numbers
options(scipen=999)

Transition rates between noun categorization systems

To test the emergence of gender we infer a global phylogeny of classifier, noun class and gender languages. First we load the noun classification data and generate data format for BayesTraits analysis that includes one multistate variable with four states: * 1 = Classifiers * 2 = NounClass * 3 = Gender * 4 = Classifiers, Gender * 5 = NounClass, Gender * 6 = Classifiers, NounClass * 7 = none

# read the data for Worldwide # Model 1
class_gender_sys <- read_excel("S2_Global_Noun_Categorization.xlsx") %>%
  # only take the columns we want
  select("Glottocode", "Classifiers", "NounClass", "Gender_Pron", "Gender_Nom") %>%
  #create a "Gender" column if a language has either pronominal or nominal gender
  mutate(Gender = Gender_Pron | Gender_Nom) %>%
  select(-Gender_Pron, -Gender_Nom) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(Systems = paste(names(across(c(-Glottocode)))[c_across(c(-Glottocode)) == TRUE], collapse = ", ")) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(Systems = if_else(Systems == "", "none", Systems)) %>%
  select(Glottocode, Systems) %>%
  # assign all systems into values of the same variable
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "Classifiers", 1)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "NounClass", 2)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "Gender", 3)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "Classifiers, Gender", 4)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "NounClass, Gender", 5)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "Classifiers, NounClass", 6)) %>%
  mutate(Systems = replace(Systems, Systems == "none", 7)) %>%
  select(Glottocode,Systems) %>%
  # change to data frame
  as.data.frame() %>%
  drop_na() #remove NAs

We load the phylogenetic trees and match them to our data

# read all the tree files
files <- list.files("all_trees/")
# read the first tree
trees <- ape::read.tree(paste0("all_trees/",files[1]))  %>%
  as.multiPhylo() 

# read all the other trees
for(i in 2:length(files)){
  # read the tree and merge it with the nexus
  trees <- c(trees,
             ape::read.tree(paste0("all_trees/",
                                   files[i]))) %>%
    # save as multiphylo object
    as.multiPhylo()
}

# only keep tip labels where we have data
trees_ww <- drop.tip.multiPhylo(trees, 
                             trees[[1]]$tip.label[!trees[[1]]$tip.label %in% class_gender_sys$Glottocode])
# only keep data for which we have the tree
data.phy.ww.class <- class_gender_sys %>% filter(Glottocode %in% trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label)
#write_tsv(data.phy.ww.class, "Noun_Categorization/data/class_genders_sys.txt")

# re-arrange the order of tip.labels so that they match across trees
trees_ww <- .compressTipLabel(trees_ww)
# random check that they match
all.equal(trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label, trees_ww[[5]]$tip.label)
## [1] TRUE
writeNexus(trees_ww %>% .uncompressTipLabel(),
           "Noun_Categorization/trees/trees_ww.nex")

We have generated the data files (.txt) and tree files (.nex) needed for running BayesTraits. The config file for running the model can be found at: “/Noun_Categorization/config/class_gender_config.txt”. The model is configured as follows:

1 # to run a multistate model 
2 # MCMC 
logfile class_gender_config-RJHPoutput
# use the reverse jump
RevJump gamma 1 1
# setting stones to get the Bayes factors
# stepping stone sampler with 100 stones and 1000 iterations per stone to estimate the marginal likelihood
Stones 100 10000
# Search for 1000000 generations, discard the first half
burnin 1000000
iterations 10000000
sample 10000
run

We load the results from the phylogenetic analysis and visualize the worldwide transition rates between Classifiers, Gender systems, and Noun Classes

class_log<- bt_read.log('Noun_Categorization/log/class_gender-RJHPoutput.Log.txt')

We visualize the results as a density plot (Fig.2)

cgs <- select(as_tibble(class_log), q12, q13, q14, q15, q16, q17, q21, q23, q24, q25, q26, q27, q31, q32, q34, q35, q36, q37, q41, q42, q43, q45, q46, q47, q51, q52, q53, q54, q56, q57, q61, q62, q63, q64, q65, q67, q71, q72, q73, q74, q75, q76) %>%
  gather() #make long format for ggplot

# Define axis labels with color highlighting
custom_labels <- c(
  'C->NC', 'C->G', 'C->C+G', 'C->NC+G', 'C->C+NC',
  "<span style='color:#F08080'>C->NONE</span>",

  'NC->C', 'NC->G', 'NC->C+G', 'NC->NC+G',
  "<span style='color:#F08080'>NC->C+NC</span>",
  'NC->NONE',

  'G->C', 'G->N', 'G->C+G', 'G->NC+G', 'G->C+NC',
  "<span style='color:#F08080'>G->NONE</span>",

  "<span style='color:#F08080'>C+G->C</span>", 'C+G->NC',
  "<span style='color:#F08080'>C+G->G</span>", 'C+G->NC+G',
  'C+G->C+NC', "<span style='color:#F08080'>C+G->NONE</span>",

  'NC+G->C', 'NC+G->NC', 'NC+G->G', 'NC+G->C+G', 'NC+G->C+NC', 'NC+G->NONE',

  'C+NC->C',
  "<span style='color:#F08080'>C+NC->NC</span>", 'C+NC->G', 'C+NC->C+G',
  'C+NC->NC+G', "<span style='color:#F08080'>C+NC->NONE</span>",

  "<span style='color:#F08080'>NONE->C</span>", 'NONE->NC', 'NONE->G',
  'NONE->C+G', 'NONE->NC+G', 'NONE->C+NC'
)

dens_plot_cgs <- ggplot(cgs,
                        aes(y = value, x = key, fill = key)) +
  ylim(c(0, 0.5)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5) +
  xlab("Transition rate") +
  ylab("Density") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "Transitions") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(2, 0.8),
    axis.text.x = element_markdown(angle = 80, hjust = 1)  # Use element_markdown!
  ) +
  ggtitle("Transition rates between Classifiers, Gender Systems and Noun Classes") +  
  scale_x_discrete(labels = custom_labels)

print(dens_plot_cgs)

ggsave("Figure2.png", dens_plot_cgs, width = 14, height = 8, dpi = 300)

Transition rates between Pronominal and Nominal gender

To test the hypothesis that nominal gender emerges from pronominal gender, we infer a global phylogenetic inference of pronominal gender, nominal gender, both pronominal and nominal gender or no gender system. We load the data, shape it into Nominal, Proniminal, Both, or None, and give them numbers for BayesTraits: 0 = No gender system 1 = Nominal gender only 2 = Pronominal gender only 3 = Both nominal and pronominal gender

ww.gen.sys <- read_excel("S3_Global_Gender.xlsx") %>%
  select("Glottocode", "System_nom", "System_pro") %>%
  mutate(Gender = case_when(
    System_nom %in% c("MF", "MN", "MFN", "FN") & System_pro == "NONE" ~ "Nominal",
    System_pro %in% c("MF", "MN", "MFN", "FN") & System_nom == "NONE" ~ "Pronominal",
    System_pro == "NONE" & System_nom == "NONE" ~ "None",
    System_nom %in% c("MF", "MN", "MFN", "FN") & System_pro %in% c("MF", "MN", "MFN", "FN") ~ "Both",
    TRUE ~ "Other"  # Just in case there's unexpected input
  )) %>%
  select(Glottocode, Gender)%>%
  mutate(Gender = case_when(
    Gender == "None"       ~ 0,
    Gender == "Nominal"    ~ 1,
    Gender == "Pronominal" ~ 2,
    Gender == "Both"       ~ 3
  ))

Again, we section out the phylogenetic trees to match the data we have and the data we have to the trees.

# only keep tip labels where we have data
trees_ww <- drop.tip.multiPhylo(trees, 
                             trees[[1]]$tip.label[!trees[[1]]$tip.label %in% ww.gen.sys$Glottocode])
# only keep data for which we have the tree
data.phy.ww.np <- ww.gen.sys %>% filter(Glottocode %in% trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label)
write_tsv(data.phy.ww.np, "Nom_Pronom/data/ww_gen_sys.txt")

# re-arrange the order of tip.labels so that they match across trees
trees_ww <- .compressTipLabel(trees_ww)
# random check that they match
all.equal(trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label, trees_ww[[5]]$tip.label)
## [1] TRUE
writeNexus(trees_ww %>% .uncompressTipLabel(),
           "Nom_Pronom/trees/trees_ww.nex")

Now we can run BayesTraits, with the config file found at: “/Nom_Pronom/config/ww_gender_sys_config.txt”. And load the log file here to plot the results (Fig.3a).

gen_log<- bt_read.log('Nom_Pronom/log/world_gender_system-RJHPoutput.Log.txt')

dens_gen_sys <- select(as_tibble(gen_log), q01, q02, q03, q10, q12, q13, q20, q21, q23, q31, q31, q32) %>%
  gather() #make long format for ggplot

dens_plot_gen <- ggplot(dens_gen_sys, aes(y = value, x = key, fill = key)) +
  ylim(c(0,0.5)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5) +
  xlab("Transition rate") +
  ylab("Density") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "Transitions") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = c(2,0.8), axis.text.x=element_text(angle=30, hjust=1)) +
  ggtitle("Transition rates between types of Gender systems") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c('None->Nominal', 'None->Pronominal', 'None->Both', 'Nominal->None', 'Nominal->Pronominal', 'Nominal->Both', 'Prnominal->None', 'Prnominal->Nominal', 'Prnominal->Both', 'Both->None', 'Both->Nominal','Both->Pronominal' ))
print(dens_plot_gen)

Transition rates between types of nominal gender systems

Next, we prepare the data for the phylogenetic analysis on the transition rates between the different types of gender systems: Masculine-Feminine, Masculine-Feminine-Neuter, Common-Neuter (here regarded as Masculine-Neuter). We change it to numeric values for BayesTraits: 1 = Masculine-Feminine 2 = Masculine-Feminine-Neuter 3 = Masculine-Neuter 4 = No nominal gender system

ww.nom.sys <- read_excel("S3_Global_Gender.xlsx") %>%
  # only take the columns we want
  select("Glottocode", "System_nom") %>%
  rename("Gender" = "System_nom") %>%
  # assign all genders into a new gender column as 4 values of the same variable
  mutate(Gender = replace(Gender, Gender == "MF", 1)) %>%
  mutate(Gender = replace(Gender, Gender == "MFN", 2)) %>%
  mutate(Gender = replace(Gender, Gender == "MN", 3)) %>%
  mutate(Gender = replace(Gender, Gender == "NONE", 4)) %>%
  select(Glottocode,Gender) %>%
  # change to data frame
  as.data.frame() %>%
  drop_na() #remove NAs

We read in the phylogenetic trees and keep only the nodes for which we have data, and only keep the data that is in the trees.

# only keep tip labels where we have data
trees_ww <- drop.tip.multiPhylo(trees, 
                             trees[[1]]$tip.label[!trees[[1]]$tip.label %in% ww.nom.sys$Glottocode])
# only keep data for which we have the tree
data.phy.ww.nom <- ww.nom.sys %>% filter(Glottocode %in% trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label)
write_tsv(data.phy.ww.nom, "Gender_Systems/data/ww_nom_sys.txt")

# re-arrange the order of tip.labels so that they match across trees
trees_ww <- .compressTipLabel(trees_ww)
# random check that they match
all.equal(trees_ww[[1]]$tip.label, trees_ww[[5]]$tip.label)
## [1] TRUE
writeNexus(trees_ww %>% .uncompressTipLabel(),
           "Gender_Systems/trees/trees_ww.nex")

Now we can run BayesTraits, with the config file found at: “/Gender_Systems/config/ww_nom_sys_config.txt”. And load the log file here to plot the results (Fig.3a).

ww_nom_log<- bt_read.log('Gender_Systems/log/world_nom-RJHPoutput.Log.txt')

gender_density_n <- select(as_tibble(ww_nom_log), q12, q13, q14, q21, q23, q24, q31, q32, q34, q41, q42, q43) %>%
  gather() #make long format for ggplot

dens_plot_nom <- ggplot(gender_density_n,
                           aes(y = value, x = key, fill = key)) +
  ylim(c(0,0.5)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5) +
  xlab("Transition rate") +
  ylab("Density") +
  scale_fill_discrete(name = "Transitions") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = c(2,0.8), axis.text.x=element_text(angle=30, hjust=1)) +
  ggtitle("Transition rates between Nominal Gender Systems") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c('MF->MFN', 'MF->MN', 'MF->NONE', 'MFN->MF', 'MFN->MN', 'MFN->NONE', 'MN->MF', 'MN->MFN', 'MN->NONE', 'NONE->MF', 'NONE->MFN', 'NONE->MN'))

print(dens_plot_nom)

Lexical analysis to evaluate the hypothesis whether semantic properties play a role in the emergence of gender systems

Here we text whether the Animacy of a noun is correlated with specific genders, and whether the noun’s belonging to the Culture or Nature realm, as defined in the paper, is correlated to specific genders.

#Animacy

# read the data
lex_gender <- read_excel("S4_Lexical_data_IE_Arawak.xlsx") %>%
  select("Family", "Language", "GlottoCode", "Concept", "ReplaceGender2", "Animacy", "Culture") %>%
  filter(ReplaceGender2 != "other") %>%
  mutate(ReplaceGender2 = ifelse(ReplaceGender2 == "N/I", "N", ReplaceGender2)) %>%
  rename(Gender = ReplaceGender2) %>%
  rename(Glottocode = GlottoCode) %>%
  drop_na()

Animate-Inanimate

lex_anim <- lex_gender %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Animacy, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Animacy) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_anim <- lex_anim %>%
  select(Animacy, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Animacy") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_anim <- chisq.test(contingency_table_anim)
print(chisq_result_anim)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_anim
## X-squared = 959.24, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_anim <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_anim$stdres))
colnames(residuals_anim) <- c("Animacy", "Gender", "Residual")

Per family analysis

This pattern of High Animacy associated with Masculine and Common nouns and Low Animacy associated with Feminine and Neuter nouns also holds when we look at each family separately.

### Arawak ###
lex_anim_ar <- lex_gender %>%
  filter(Family == "Arawak") %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Animacy, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Animacy) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_anim_ar <- lex_anim_ar %>%
  select(Animacy, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Animacy") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_anim_ar <- chisq.test(contingency_table_anim_ar)
print(chisq_result_anim_ar)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_anim_ar
## X-squared = 831.78, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_anim_ar <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_anim_ar$stdres))
colnames(residuals_anim_ar) <- c("Animacy", "Gender", "Residual")

# Plot standardized residuals with numbers
ggplot(residuals_anim_ar, aes(x = Gender, y = Animacy, fill = Residual)) +
  geom_tile(color = "grey95", size = 0.1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(Residual, 1)),
    size = 3, color = "gray30", family = "Georgia"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#b39ddb", mid = "white", high = "#80cbc4", midpoint = 0,
    name = "Std. Residual"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Arawak Standardized Residuals: Gender Distribution for Animacy",
    x = "Gender", y = "Concept"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Georgia") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

### Indo-European ###
lex_anim_ie <- lex_gender %>%
  filter(Family == "Indo-European") %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Animacy, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Animacy) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_anim_ie <- lex_anim_ie %>%
  select(Animacy, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Animacy") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_anim_ie <- chisq.test(contingency_table_anim_ie)
print(chisq_result_anim_ie)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_anim_ie
## X-squared = 474.32, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_anim_ie <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_anim_ie$stdres))
colnames(residuals_anim_ie) <- c("Animacy", "Gender", "Residual")

# Plot standardized residuals with numbers
ggplot(residuals_anim_ie, aes(x = Gender, y = Animacy, fill = Residual)) +
  geom_tile(color = "grey95", size = 0.1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(Residual, 1)),
    size = 3, color = "gray30", family = "Georgia"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#b39ddb", mid = "white", high = "#80cbc4", midpoint = 0,
    name = "Std. Residual"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Indo-European Standardized Residuals: Gender Distribution for Animacy",
    x = "Gender", y = "Concept"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Georgia") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

Culture - Nature

lex_cul_nat <- lex_gender %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Culture, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Culture) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_cul <- lex_cul_nat %>%
  select(Culture, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Culture") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_cul <- chisq.test(contingency_table_cul)
print(chisq_result_cul)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_cul
## X-squared = 384.63, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_cul <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_cul$stdres))
colnames(residuals_cul) <- c("Culture", "Gender", "Residual")

Per family analysis

The pattern we see in the analysis of both family mainly hold for each family alone as well. Nature nouns are correlated with Masculine and Common and Culture nouns with Neuter and Feminine, though in the Indo-European family, there is no significant effect for the Feminine gender.

### Arawak ###
lex_cul_nat_ar <- lex_gender %>%
  filter(Family == "Arawak") %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Culture, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Culture) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_cul_ar <- lex_cul_nat_ar %>%
  select(Culture, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Culture") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_cul_ar <- chisq.test(contingency_table_cul_ar)
print(chisq_result_cul_ar)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_cul_ar
## X-squared = 326.91, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_cul_ar <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_cul_ar$stdres))
colnames(residuals_cul_ar) <- c("Culture", "Gender", "Residual")

# Plot standardized residuals with numbers
ggplot(residuals_cul_ar, aes(x = Gender, y = Culture, fill = Residual)) +
  geom_tile(color = "grey95", size = 0.1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(Residual, 1)),
    size = 3, color = "gray30", family = "Georgia"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#b39ddb", mid = "white", high = "#80cbc4", midpoint = 0,
    name = "Std. Residual"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Arawak Standardized Residuals: Gender Distribution for Culture - Nature",
    x = "Gender", y = "Concept"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Georgia") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

### Indo-European ###
lex_cul_nat_ie <- lex_gender %>%
  filter(Family == "Indo-European") %>%
  mutate(Gender = recode(Gender,
                         "C" = "Common",
                         "F" = "Feminine",
                         "M" = "Masculine",
                         "N" = "Neuter")) %>%
  mutate(Gender = factor(Gender, levels = rev(c("Masculine", "Feminine", "Neuter", "Common")))) %>%
  count(Culture, Gender) %>%
  group_by(Culture) %>%
  mutate(Percentage = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

contingency_table_cul_ie <- lex_cul_nat_ie %>%
  select(Culture, Gender, n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Gender, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  column_to_rownames("Culture") %>%
  as.matrix()

# Run chi-square test
chisq_result_cul_ie <- chisq.test(contingency_table_cul_ie)
print(chisq_result_cul_ie)
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  contingency_table_cul_ie
## X-squared = 235.59, df = 3, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
# Extract standardized residuals
residuals_cul_ie <- as.data.frame(as.table(chisq_result_cul_ie$stdres))
colnames(residuals_cul_ie) <- c("Culture", "Gender", "Residual")

# Plot standardized residuals with numbers
ggplot(residuals_cul_ie, aes(x = Gender, y = Culture, fill = Residual)) +
  geom_tile(color = "grey95", size = 0.1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(Residual, 1)),
    size = 3, color = "gray30", family = "Georgia"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#b39ddb", mid = "white", high = "#80cbc4", midpoint = 0,
    name = "Std. Residual"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Indo-European Standardized Residuals: Gender Distribution for Culture - Nature",
    x = "Gender", y = "Concept"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Georgia") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, size = 10, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

Plot both Animacy and Culture together for paper:

# Standardize and add Category column
residuals_anim <- residuals_anim %>%
  rename(Dimension = Animacy) %>%
  mutate(Category = Dimension) %>%   # Store original Animacy value
  select(Category, Gender, Residual, Dimension)

residuals_cul <- residuals_cul %>%
  rename(Dimension = Culture) %>%
  mutate(Category = Dimension) %>%   # Store original Culture value
  select(Category, Gender, Residual, Dimension)

residuals_both <- bind_rows(residuals_cul, residuals_anim)

ggplot(residuals_both, aes(x = Gender, y = Category, fill = Residual)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Residual, 1)), size = 2, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#b39ddb", mid = "white", high = "#80cbc4", midpoint = 0,
    name = "Std. Residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Georgia") +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),        
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6, face = "bold"),  
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6),                  
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
     legend.position = "none"
  )

ggsave("figure4.png", width = 6, height = 2, dpi = 300)
